title: Why Core Web Vitals Are a Revenue Problem, Not Just an SEO One
dek: Most teams treat Core Web Vitals as a search-ranking checkbox. The field data tells a different story: loading speed and visual stability are quietly setting a ceiling on how many visitors convert.

title: What Edge Caching Actually Buys You (Beyond 'It's Faster')
dek: Edge caching is usually pitched as a speed upgrade. The more useful way to think about it is as an origin-load and reliability strategy — one that happens to make pages faster as a side effect.

title: Serverless Doesn't Always Save Money — Here's When It Actually Does
dek: Pay-per-execution sounds like a universal cost win. The real picture has a crossover point: serverless is cheaper below it and more expensive above it, and most teams never check which side they're on.

title: Having a Backup Isn't the Same as Having a Disaster Recovery Plan
dek: Most organizations back up their data. Far fewer ever test whether that backup actually restores. The gap between those two facts is where outages turn into business-ending events.

title: Technical Debt Isn't a Code Quality Problem — It's a Budget Line You Can't See
dek: Most leadership teams have never seen 'technical debt' on a budget line. That doesn't mean it isn't there — it means it's hiding inside every delayed feature and every estimate that quietly doubled.

title: Most API Integration Budgets Are Wrong Before the Project Starts
dek: Quote an API project by counting endpoints and you'll be wrong by 30-40%. The cost lives in the parts that never make it into the initial scope: documentation, error handling, versioning, and the integration work nobody priced.

title: Most Digital Transformation Projects Don't Fail Because of the Technology
dek: Roughly 70% of digital transformation initiatives miss their goals. Across every major study on why, the technology itself is rarely the reason — the sequencing of the work usually is.

title: Your CRM, ERP, and Support Tool Are Lying to Each Other — Here's the Bill
dek: No one decides to build data silos. They accumulate one reasonable tool purchase at a time, until sales, finance, and support are each working from a different version of the truth.
